does: This paper formulates Safe Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback as an infinite-horizon discounted Constrained Markov Decision Process rather than a finite-horizon problem, addressing the reality that users interact with language models over continuing sequences. The authors propose two primal-dual policy gradient algorithms that directly optimize policies from human preference data without fitting intermediate reward models and accommodate variable-length trajectories. Both algorithms achieve global convergence guarantees with polynomial rates measured in policy gradient iterations, trajectory sample lengths, and the number of human preference queries needed, providing the first theoretical analysis of infinite-horizon Safe RLHF with formal convergence bounds.
connects: This work extends the emerging Safe RLHF paradigm, which has successfully decoupled helpfulness and harmlessness objectives in large language model training through human feedback. It responds to limitations in prior empirical approaches that treat trajectories as fixed-length episodes and rely on fitting separate reward models—a two-stage pipeline that can be inefficient and theoretically difficult to analyze. The paper builds on constrained reinforcement learning theory and primal-dual optimization methods, applying these classical tools to the new challenge of learning from human preferences in an ongoing interaction setting. It connects to broader advances in reinforcement learning from human feedback and theoretical foundations for safe RL, grounding what were previously empirical successes in rigorous optimization theory.
enables: This theoretical framework opens pathways for developing more principled, safer deployment of language models by ensuring formal convergence guarantees during preference-based training. The flexibility to handle variable-length trajectories and avoid reward model fitting could lead to more sample-efficient and direct training procedures. The infinite-horizon formulation enables future work on continual learning scenarios where models must adapt safely over extended interaction periods. The polynomial convergence rates establish benchmarks for algorithm design and may inspire practical improvements to Safe RLHF implementation. Additionally, the primal-dual approach could be extended to handle richer constraint structures or multiple competing objectives, moving toward more nuanced representations of human safety preferences and enabling deployment in domains requiring stricter safety guarantees.
